title: The Heart Attack

jokes.text: As an adult, if I want a cookie, I have a cookie, okay?
jokes.punchline: The simple freedom of eating cookies whenever you want
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor about mundane adult freedoms. Well-structured and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The simple freedom of eating cookies whenever you want. That's what being an adult is all about. You know, as a kid, your parents control everything. 'No cookies before dinner.' 'Finish your vegetables.' But now? Now I can have cookies for breakfast, cookies for lunch, cookies for dinner. I'm living the dream.

jokes.text: I have three cookies or four cookies... or 11 cookies if I want.
jokes.setup: Building on the cookie freedom theme
jokes.punchline: The absurd escalation to 11 cookies
jokes.explanation: Good escalation technique, though the number choice feels somewhat arbitrary.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now I'm thinking, why stop at 10? Why not 11 cookies? I'm a free man! I can have 11 cookies if I want. That's the beauty of it. Nobody's counting. Well, except me. I'm counting. But I'm counting freely.

jokes.text: Many times, I will intentionally ruin my entire appetite. Just ruin it.
jokes.punchline: The deliberate self-sabotage aspect with repetition for emphasis
jokes.explanation: Captures the petty rebelliousness of adulthood. The repetition of 'ruin it' adds emphasis.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the problem is with being an adult? You're free to make terrible decisions. And the worst part? You can make the same terrible decision over and over again. Nobody stops you. There's no parent going, 'Hey, didn't you already do that?' No. You just keep doing it.

jokes.text: And then I call my mother up right after to tell her that I did it.
jokes.punchline: The childish need to confess/brag to his mother
jokes.explanation: Brilliant insight into adult psychology - we're still seeking parental approval even in rebellion. Perfect economy and character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I have to call my mother and tell her what I did.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because I'm like a little boy. I do something wrong, I have this need to confess to my mother.

jokes.text: Hello, Mom? Yeah, I just ruined my entire appetite. Cookies.
jokes.punchline: The mock phone call with deadpan delivery
jokes.explanation: The single word 'Cookies' as explanation is perfectly economical and funny. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My mother called me up, she says, 'I have a confession to make.' I said, 'What?' She goes, 'I never liked your girlfriend.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I said, 'Ma, that's not a confession, that's a statement of fact. You've been telling me this for years.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She says, 'Well, I wanted it to be official.' So I said, 'Okay, consider it official. Anything else?' She goes, 'No, that's it.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I hung up the phone. I'm thinking, what am I supposed to do with this information? Change my entire life based on my mother's confession?

jokes.text: As an adult, we understand even if you ruin an appetite... there's another appetite coming right behind it.
jokes.setup: Explaining the philosophy behind appetite ruining
jokes.punchline: Treating appetites like an endless renewable resource
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist logic about appetite as commodity. Good Seinfeld-style overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I think we treat appetites like an endless renewable resource. Like we're never going to run out. But the truth is, appetite is finite. Once it's gone, it's gone. You can't just keep taking and taking without consequences.

jokes.text: Look, Zigmond. Look at the sky! The planet's on fire! It is just as you prophesised!
jokes.setup: Dramatic B-movie dialogue playing on TV
jokes.punchline: The overwrought melodrama and terrible acting
jokes.explanation: Standard bad TV parody. Sets up the later callback but not particularly original on its own.

dialogue.speaker: TV Actor
dialogue.line: I can't believe you've done this to me. After all we've meant to each other, how could you betray me like this?

dialogue.speaker: TV Actress
dialogue.line: I had no choice. He forced me to do it. I never wanted to hurt you.

dialogue.speaker: TV Actor
dialogue.line: Forced you? FORCED YOU?! There's nothing in this world that could force me to believe that lie!

jokes.text: You're bringing in an outside cucumber?
jokes.setup: George has brought a cucumber to the coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Treating it like contraband with 'outside cucumber'
jokes.explanation: Great observational humor about restaurant policies. 'Outside cucumber' is perfectly economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: This is an outside cucumber.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's an outside cucumber?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A cucumber from outside. You can't bring outside food into Monk's.

jokes.text: What have I done? What...? I can't read this. 'Fullman hurtel vom'?
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to read his late-night notes
jokes.punchline: The illegible nonsense words
jokes.explanation: Relatable comedian struggle. The made-up words are funny but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm trying to read my notes from last night. 'Fragile ... Giddy ... Stockings?' What was I thinking?

jokes.text: Fax me some halibut.
jokes.setup: Jerry trying to decipher his notes
jokes.punchline: The nonsensical phrase that emerged from illegible writing
jokes.explanation: Absurd and memorable. The combination of 'fax' and 'halibut' is unexpectedly funny and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm trying to read my notes from last night. 'Master of the house'? I don't remember writing that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, is that 'master'... or 'bastard'? No, that can't be right either.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh! 'Master of my domain!' That's it. That's the whole bit.

jokes.text: Is that funny? Is that a joke?
jokes.punchline: The meta-commentary on comedy itself
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about the comedy process. Very Jerry-like self-doubt.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What's the deal with questioning your own material? You write a joke, you tell the joke, you move on. You don't sit there and analyze it like you're some kind of comedy scientist.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But then you think, 'Wait, is this actually funny? Or am I just saying it's funny because I wrote it?' And now you're trapped in this meta-loop where you can't enjoy your own comedy anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the real joke—the comedian becoming the audience of his own act. Which, by the way, is not funny. That's just sad.

jokes.text: That's like asking, 'Where's Waldo?'
jokes.punchline: Comparing Kramer's unpredictability to the hidden character game
jokes.explanation: Perfect character observation about Kramer. The pop culture reference works well and is economical.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Where's Kramer?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't know. He's like that hidden character game — you never know where he's going to pop up.

jokes.text: I think I'm having a heart attack.
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic hypochondriac declaration
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - hypochondriac tendency delivered with perfect timing and integration.

jokes.text: I think what he's trying to say... is that he's having a heart attack.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's overly literal clarification
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man delivery. Elaine's literalness contrasts well with the chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He means he's not *literally* a marine biologist, Jerry. He's just saying that to sound impressive.

jokes.text: You saw that show on PBS last night, Coronary Country.
jokes.punchline: Revealing it's just hypochondria from watching medical shows
jokes.explanation: Great character insight about George's suggestibility. 'Coronary Country' is a funny fake show name.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what did the doctor say?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He says there's nothing wrong with me. It's all in my head.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're fine?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm fine? I'm dying, Jerry! I watched this thing on the Discovery Channel about a man whose heart exploded.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: His heart exploded?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It just burst. One day he's making a sandwich, the next day boom — he's all over the kitchen.

jokes.text: I saw it in the TV Guide, I called him... told him make sure and not watch it.
jokes.setup: Jerry knew George would be affected
jokes.punchline: The futile attempt at prevention
jokes.explanation: Shows Jerry knows George well enough to predict his reactions. Good character work.

jokes.text: He saw that show on anorexia last year... ate like an animal for two weeks.
jokes.setup: Establishing George's pattern of medical show reactions
jokes.punchline: The opposite reaction to anorexia show - overeating
jokes.explanation: Perfect George characterization - his extreme overcompensation. The irony is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're watching TV, there's a show about anorexia, and you're eating a sandwich.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know, I know. It's not right.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What do you do? You can't eat the sandwich.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I put it down.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You put down the sandwich. But then the show goes off, and three minutes later, you're eating a herring.

jokes.text: Why can't I have a heart attack?! I'm allowed.
jokes.punchline: Treating heart attacks like a civil right
jokes.explanation: Classic George logic - indignation about being denied something terrible. Perfect character integration and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's not a lie if you believe it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm having a heart attack!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're not having a heart attack.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How do you know? You're not a doctor. This is discrimination! You can't just decide who is and isn't having a heart attack. It's a civil right!

jokes.text: Manhattan Memorial. Less of a line.
jokes.setup: Choosing which hospital to go to
jokes.punchline: Treating hospital emergency rooms like retail establishments
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about healthcare as consumer experience. Very economical and George-like practicality.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't understand. Why do we have to go to St. Vincent's? I like the emergency room at Mt. Sinai better.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? They're hospitals, not shoe stores.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So? I like the facilities better. The waiting area is nicer. They have better magazines.

jokes.text: I never been in the hospital a day in my life... except when I had my tonsils out.
jokes.setup: George claiming he's never been in a hospital
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself
jokes.explanation: Good George self-contradiction. Sets up the tonsils storyline well.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I've never been in a hospital in my life.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What about when you were born?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, I was born at home.

jokes.text: You know, they never gave me any ice cream. I always felt that if they...
jokes.punchline: Getting cut off before sharing his grievance
jokes.explanation: Shows George's tendency to hold grudges about minor things. The interruption adds to it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was in the hospital for my tonsillectomy, and the doctor comes in, he says, 'George, we're gonna have to remove your tonsils.' I said, 'Doctor, I don't think you understand the gravity of this situation. When I was a kid, I had this experience with my tonsils that really—'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, nobody cares about your tonsils.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: See, that's what the doctor said!

jokes.text: Shut up!
jokes.punchline: Jerry's blunt interruption
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. Jerry's impatience with George's tangents is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, I was thinking about ice cream the other day. It's such a perfect food. You can have it any time of year, any time of day. Summer, winter, doesn't matter. And the flavors! Vanilla, chocolate, strawberry, mint chip... I mean, who comes up with these combinations?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, nobody cares.

jokes.text: 'Salami salami bologna.' Definitely.
jokes.punchline: The nonsensical medical interpretation
jokes.explanation: Absurdist medical nonsense delivered with complete authority. Very quotable and unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: These results are highly unusual. Your heart appears to be shaped like a pastrami sandwich.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Is that bad?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Well, it's delicious, but medically speaking, it's a concern.

jokes.text: 'Salami salami bologna'?
jokes.punchline: Jerry's confused repetition
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man reaction to the absurd medical reading.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You have a severe case of network sitcom syndrome.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Network sitcom syndrome?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes, it's very common in New York comedians.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Network sitcom syndrome... I've never heard of it.

jokes.text: Well, you know, I was thinking, if things don't exactly... work out...
jokes.punchline: Euphemistically asking for his stuff if he dies
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about death with Kramer's characteristic opportunism and awkward phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So if anything happens to you, I get the jacket, right?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? Why would you get my jacket?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, you know, if you die or something.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm not dying, Kramer.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, hypothetically, if you were to pass... the jacket comes to me.

jokes.text: Well, it wouldn't fit you. The sleeves are too short.
jokes.punchline: George's practical objection despite being supposedly dying
jokes.explanation: Perfect George - even when dying, he's practical and slightly petty. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: George, I want the jacket.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? You can't have the jacket. I'm not dead yet.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Come on, you're dying. What do you need it for?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I might get cold.

jokes.text: do you think it'd be all right if I called Susan Davis?
jokes.punchline: Now asking about George's romantic prospects too
jokes.explanation: Escalates the morbid opportunism. Very Kramer-like lack of social boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So what's the deal with the jacket?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What do you mean, 'what's the deal'? It's a jacket.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but where'd you get it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I bought it. At a store.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: And while we're at it, what about you and that woman from the office? You ever gonna ask her out?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Kramer, I don't need you asking about my love life.

jokes.text: Well, it's not like we'll be bumping into you.
jokes.setup: George's shock at the Susan Davis request
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact explanation
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and logical in Kramer's twisted way. Perfect economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, Susan Davis wants me to be in her wedding?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, she's been talking about it for weeks.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But I barely know her!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So? She likes you.

jokes.text: Jerry... kill me. Kill me now. I'm begging you.
jokes.punchline: The dramatic request for euthanasia
jokes.explanation: George's characteristic overdramatics. The repetition adds to the desperation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just kill me. Kill me now. I can't take this anymore. I'm begging you, just end it all.

jokes.text: Well... What, kind of like this?
jokes.punchline: Jerry actually miming smothering him with a pillow
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag. Jerry's casual consideration of the request is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Just kill me. Put me out of my misery.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: *mimes smothering George with a pillow*

jokes.text: Jerk-off.
jokes.setup: Elaine arriving to see Jerry with the pillow
jokes.punchline: Her blunt assessment of Jerry's behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Elaine directness and timing. Maximum economy with one word.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What is wrong with you? You're like a little child with a security blanket!

jokes.text: Is anybody getting your apartment?
jokes.punchline: Immediately asking about his apartment like everyone else
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the vulture-like behavior. Shows even Elaine is thinking practically.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Hey George, how are you feeling?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm terrible, I feel awful.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, that's too bad. So, your apartment... is it rent-controlled?

jokes.text: Hey, is anybody listening?!
jokes.setup: George talking about changing his life
jokes.punchline: Shouting in frustration about being ignored
jokes.explanation: Good George frustration moment. The irony of zen followed by anger is nice.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm gonna do it. I'm gonna walk over there and I'm gonna talk to her.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Hello. My name is George. I'm unemployed and I live with my parents.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My name is Jerry.

jokes.text: Oh, God. Mommy.
jokes.setup: Doctor about to give test results
jokes.punchline: George reverting to childlike fear
jokes.explanation: Perfect George vulnerability. The regression to 'Mommy' is both funny and pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I have your test results.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Am I gonna die?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: We all are eventually.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But soon? Like, before I finish this coffee?

jokes.text: Well, I wouldn't go that far.
jokes.setup: George celebrating that nothing's wrong with him
jokes.punchline: Jerry's cruel but accurate correction
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry deadpan cruelty. Perfect timing and very quotable putdown.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Everything's fine. There's nothing wrong with me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's plenty wrong with you.

jokes.text: Well, they've grown back.
jokes.setup: George asking about his childhood tonsillectomy
jokes.punchline: The impossible medical development
jokes.explanation: Absurdist medical humor. The casual delivery of impossible news is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Doctor, I was wondering about my tonsillectomy when I was a kid. Do you have any records of that?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Well, let me check your file here. Yes, I see it. You had your tonsils removed when you were eight years old.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Right, right. So they're definitely gone, correct? I mean, they can't grow back or anything?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: No, George. Once tonsils are removed, they don't grow back. That's permanent.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Because I've been having this problem, and I was thinking maybe... they grew back?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: George, that's medically impossible. Tonsils do not regenerate in humans.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So you're telling me I'm just stuck with whatever this is?

jokes.text: Yeah, if you've been exposed to gamma rays.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's comic book reference to explain the impossibility
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture callback to comic book science. Jerry's nerd knowledge showing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't regrow your tonsils. Once they're out, they're out. It's like... it's like if you burned your comic books, you can't just grow new ones.

jokes.text: In fact, we were forbidden to socialize with anybody... who didn't have their tonsils.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's family's extreme tonsil orthodoxy
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist escalation. The idea of tonsil-based discrimination is wonderfully silly.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: My father had his tonsils out when he was a kid. My mother refused to have hers removed. She said they were part of God's design.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So my parents couldn't kiss. For thirty years, they couldn't kiss because of tonsils.

jokes.text: And we were forbidden to socialize with tonsil people.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's family had the opposite equally extreme policy
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and callback. The term 'tonsil people' is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My family had the opposite policy. My parents wouldn't take my tonsils out no matter what. I could have had a tonsil hanging down my throat like a uvula and they'd be like, 'Leave it alone, it's probably just a phase.'

jokes.text: Well, it's like the Capulets and the Montagues.
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to Romeo and Juliet's feuding families
jokes.explanation: Brilliant literary reference that perfectly caps the absurd family feud concept. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's like Romeo and Juliet, except instead of feuding families, it's competing tonsil removal philosophies.

jokes.text: Women go after doctors like men go after models. They want someone with knowledge of the body. We just want the body.
jokes.punchline: The contrast between intellectual vs. physical attraction
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational humor with perfect structure. Very quotable and insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's interesting about attraction? It's like your brain and your body are having two completely different conversations. Your brain's like, 'She's intelligent, well-read, fascinating person.' Your body's like, 'Yeah, yeah, but look at her shoes.'

jokes.text: It's like Sizzler opened up a hospital.
jokes.punchline: The bizarre comparison to a chain restaurant
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kramer observation. The specificity of 'Sizzler' makes it funnier and more quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, this hospital cafeteria is amazing. The food is incredible. It's like eating at a four-star restaurant.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, it's a hospital cafeteria.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You don't understand. This is better than Applebee's.

jokes.text: They gonna do a zipper job? Oh, they love to do zipper jobs.
jokes.punchline: His casual, conspiracy-minded medical terminology
jokes.explanation: Very Kramer paranoia and colorful language. 'Zipper job' is a great invented term.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Surgery? What kind of surgery?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: They're gonna go in there with a scalpel, start poking around. Who knows what they're gonna find? Could be anything. They might nick something, sever a nerve. You could end up a vegetable.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I knew a guy, went in for a simple procedure. Came out with a permanent limp. The doctors, they don't tell you these things.

jokes.text: You get that heart pumping... Suddenly, boom! Next thing you know... you got a hose coming out of your chest attached to a piece of luggage.
jokes.setup: Warning about heart problems and sex
jokes.punchline: The vivid, disturbing medical imagery
jokes.explanation: Kramer's vivid, paranoid medical imagery. The 'piece of luggage' detail is particularly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me I can't have sex because of my heart?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's what the doctor said. He showed me this chart of the human heart during intercourse. It was like a crime scene in there.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A crime scene?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Blood everywhere, valves slamming shut like prison doors. I couldn't unsee it, Jerry. I couldn't unsee it.

jokes.text: Now he's sitting in a chair by a window going: 'My name is Bob!'
jokes.setup: Story about Bob Sacamano's botched surgery
jokes.punchline: The tragic outcome delivered with Kramer's casual tone
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about medical malpractice. The simple 'My name is Bob!' is tragicomic.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, Bob had this surgery, right? And the guy, he nicked something. Now Bob's got this weird thing going on with his side. It's like, he's all lopsided now.

jokes.text: No, Johnny Yuma was a rebel. Ackman is a nut.
jokes.setup: Kramer calling the healer a rebel
jokes.punchline: Jerry's distinction using a TV Western reference
jokes.explanation: Great pop culture reference that serves the joke well. Good distinction between rebel and nut.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: He's a rebel.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's not a rebel. A rebel is someone who fights against the system. He's just a guy who doesn't follow the rules. There's a difference. A rebel would be like the Magnificent Seven. This guy is just... a guy.

jokes.text: First visit? 38 bucks.
jokes.punchline: The ridiculously low alternative medicine price
jokes.explanation: Good economic contrast. The specific price point makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Four thousand dollars? Jerry, I know a guy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know a guy? What guy?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: A guy who does acupuncture out of his apartment. Fifty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Fifty bucks? Kramer, this is a hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So? It's the same thing.

jokes.text: I'm 33, I haven't outgrown the problems of puberty... I'm already facing the problems of old age.
jokes.punchline: The idea of skipping healthy adulthood entirely
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George self-analysis. The concept of skipping healthy adulthood is universally relatable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm tired of all this healthy living. Eating right, exercising, getting enough sleep. What's it all for? So I can live longer? I don't want to live longer, I want to live better. Why can't I just skip ahead? Go straight from childhood to old age. Get right to the good stuff.

jokes.text: I went from having orgasms immediately to taking forever. You can do your taxes in the time it takes me to have an orgasm.
jokes.punchline: The specific, uncomfortable comparison to tax preparation
jokes.explanation: Peak George oversharing with perfect specificity. The tax comparison is brilliantly absurd and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's like doing my taxes. I sit down, I'm dreading it, I know it's going to be painful, there's forms I don't understand, and by the end of it I'm confused and angry and I've lost money.

jokes.text: I never had a really good pickle.
jokes.punchline: The random, mundane complaint about pickles
jokes.explanation: Perfect George non-sequitur. The specificity and randomness make it very quotable and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And another thing - why do pickles always slip out of the jar? You reach in there with a fork, and they just shoot across the counter like little green hockey pucks!

jokes.text: Your parents would have been well-advised to wait.
jokes.setup: George saying he was born in April
jokes.punchline: The healer's matter-of-fact criticism of his birth timing
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan insult delivered with holistic authority. The formal phrasing makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was born in April.

dialogue.speaker: The Healer
dialogue.line: April? That's the worst month to be born in.

jokes.text: And you're not a doctor, but you play one in real life.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's twist on the famous commercial tagline
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion of the classic commercial line. Perfect Jerry-style wordplay and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: An apple a day keeps the doctor away. You know what? An apple a day keeps the doctor away... if the doctor is evil.

jokes.text: There's some hot air blowing in here.
jokes.setup: Healer talking about 'invasion of heat and wind'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's double entendre about the healer's BS
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay on the healer's terminology. Jerry's skepticism shining through.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: An invasion of heat and wind? That sounds like my last relationship.

jokes.text: I love cramp bark.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's fake enthusiasm for the gross-sounding ingredient
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan sarcasm. 'Cramp bark' is inherently funny to say.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that sounds great! What else is in it?

dialogue.speaker: Healer
dialogue.line: Essence of bark, a pinch of tree fungus, and a little bit of moss.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Moss? You're putting moss in my tea? What am I, a stone?

jokes.text: Cleaver? I once had cleaver as a kid. I was able to lift a car.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's mishearing and absurd strength claim
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay on cleavers/cleaver. The car lifting is absurd enough to work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Wait, what herb is that?

dialogue.speaker: Healer
dialogue.line: Cleavers.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cleavers? I thought you said 'Chivas.' I was about to say, that's a Scotch!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You take that herb and suddenly you can lift a car with one hand.

jokes.text: Couch grass and cramp bark? I think that's what killed Curly.
jokes.punchline: Blaming the Three Stooges member's death on herbs
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture reference with dark humor. The specificity of Curly makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm reading the label on this herbal tea, right? And it's got all these ingredients - echinacea, ginseng, St. John's Wort... And I'm thinking, this killed Moe.

jokes.text: Cleveland, 117. San Antonio, 109.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking about his illegible note
jokes.punchline: The healer reading it as basketball scores
jokes.explanation: Great callback and misdirection. The mundane sports scores are perfect anticlimax.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What does this say? I can't read my own handwriting.

dialogue.speaker: Tor
dialogue.line: Let me see that. Hmm, 76 to 89, Knicks over the Celtics.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's not basketball scores, that's my grocery list!

jokes.text: I'm an eggplant! I'm a vegetable!
jokes.punchline: His allergic reaction and vegetable identification
jokes.explanation: Absurd and quotable reaction. The specificity of 'eggplant' makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What is this stuff? It tastes like a meadow.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh no, oh no... I can't breathe. I'm having an allergic reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: George, what's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The tea! There's something in here... is that a turnip? That's a turnip! Who puts a turnip in tea?!

jokes.text: All this over a Chuckle.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's summary of the absurd situation
jokes.explanation: Good observational summary. The understatement works well given the chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm in the ambulance, and the guy's telling me about his back injury. I'm thinking, 'This is great, I'm getting a personal health history from a guy who's supposed to be saving my life.' Then he starts complaining about his chiropractor. I said, 'You know what? Maybe you should try a different chiropractor.' He goes, 'That's what my wife said!' So now I'm giving medical advice in an ambulance. That's my new thing. I'm Dr. Seinfeld.

jokes.text: It's a jelly candy. Comes in five flavours.
jokes.setup: Someone asking what a Chuckle is
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact explanation amid chaos
jokes.explanation: Decent deadpan moment. Jerry staying factual during crisis is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A Chuckle? It's a candy. You know, the little round ones with the different flavors - cherry, orange, lemon. You suck on it, it changes color as you go down to the center.

jokes.text: A kiss? With the tongue? The glossa with the bumps and the papillae?
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to kiss Jerry goodnight
jokes.punchline: Jerry's medical terminology making kissing gross
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the medical terminology. Makes kissing sound disgusting and clinical.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Come on, just one little kiss goodnight.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no. Do you realize what you're asking? You're asking me to take my lips, which are mucous membranes, and press them against your mucous membranes.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's disgusting. We're basically rubbing our open sores together.

jokes.text: I don't want to run into Dr. Tongue.
jokes.setup: Elaine explaining why she can't stay long
jokes.punchline: Nickname for the doctor based on his tongue obsession
jokes.explanation: Good callback with a funny nickname. Economical and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can't stay long, I have a doctor's appointment.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, my new doctor. He's got this weird thing where he's always looking at your tongue. I mean, always. For everything.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I went in for a broken arm, he wanted to see my tongue.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So I'm calling him 'The Tongue.' That's his nickname now. Dr. The Tongue.

jokes.text: That's it! That's it! 'Flaming globes of Zigmond'! That's my note. That's what I thought was so funny.
jokes.setup: Jerry finally understanding his illegible note
jokes.punchline: The revelation that it was just a bad TV show quote
jokes.explanation: Great callback that ties the episode together. The anticlimax is perfect for Jerry's character.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh my God, I finally figured out what this says!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's a quote from a TV show.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A really bad TV show.

jokes.text: It's not funny. There's nothing funny about that.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's blunt assessment that it's not funny
jokes.explanation: Perfect Elaine bluntness. The repetition emphasizes the harsh verdict.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It's not funny.

jokes.text: I had a heart attack this big... but I gave myself one of these: Clear! And it's gone.
jokes.setup: Jerry's standup about future medical advances
jokes.punchline: Self-defibrillation as casual as taking aspirin
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with physical element. The casual 'Clear!' is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I think is coming? Self-defibrillation. Just like taking an aspirin. You feel a little flutter in your chest, you reach into your pocket, pull out your personal defibrillator, and ZAP! Right there in the coffee shop. 'Excuse me, just shocking myself back to life. Anybody got a napkin?'

tmdb_overview: George thinks he's had a heart attack. The doctor tells him otherwise, but he might want to get his tonsils and adenoids removed. Elaine is interested in the doctor and he is interested in her tongue. George not wanting to deal with the cost, follows Kramer's suggestion of going to a holistic healer. The healer's cure turns George purple and the ride to the hospital is delayed over a Chuckle.
insight: George's hypochondriac spiral drives 65 jokes across 22 minutes—3.32 per minute peak density.
